item.title: hybrids
item.content:encoded:
I'd just like to point out that if this thing can be conclusively classified into a known non-anomalous genus, then chances are it would be able to breed with and produce (almost certainly non-fertile) offspring with other members of the Accipiter genus, just as horses and donkeys being in the same genus allows them to breed and produce mules (if the donkey is the dad) and hinneys (if the horse is the dad).

Whether or how you want to approach this is of course up to you, but I personally foresee the foundation breeding an entire cyborg air force of literal war hawks.

Food for thought.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image originates from this website, whose photographer has not explicitly released it under a CC BY-SA 3.0 license. As such, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It shouldn't give me the feels, no, it shouldn't. This is just a plane-shaped bird, that's all.

it will flinch when tissue samples are taken, tremble during thunderstorms, and bounce up and down when its tether is being attached prior to flight sessions

… that doesn't remind me of the little happy bundle of fur and joy that was by my side for 8 years. Not at all. *sniff*

I'm at goddamn work, can't cry now.
Take your + and leave me alone you sneaky feel-skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's so much to like here. The horror of being digested by a seatbelt, the cute little personality of the plane… I like this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just don't see why it can't have ear holes, or eyes. Having it somehow able to see and hear makes it seem magical to me.

Oh, it probably does have ear- and eye-equivalents somewhere. We just haven't been able to figure out where or what they are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Totally agree here!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am just happy it was not made of peoples.

how many pilots do you think this creature ate before the Foundation took it into custody?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a birdplane, a motherfuckin' birdplane...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I was rereading this because I remember liking the article a lot. And then I decided to look up hawks, then hawk cries, and I found this video of a hawk screaming.

The first 7 seconds accurately describe my experience writing academic papers. Hawks are amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is hilariously adorable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did anyone else think of 1387? No? Just me. Okay. That's not important, just a random thought. This is the thing that gets me about this sort of item. It "somehow" can see and hear. I know you have to be open to accept a literal bird plane (cue the Key of Awesome) I just don't see why it can't have ear holes, or eyes. Having it somehow able to see and hear makes it seem magical to me. Not enough to stop my upvote. Just wondering if anyone else ever feels this way?

item.title: I like this!
item.content:encoded: If I had to give an SCP the title "adorable", this one would take the cake. I mean, the thought of this thing being playful is somewhat funny. To think it broke containment, flew about for a few minutes, then surrendered itself cooperatively. Makes you think it is like a domestic animal, wanting to roam about. Yet is knows it needs it's owners for survival, and as a result is aware to obey (most) commands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the most bizarrely adorable thing I've read in awhile. Nice to see some anomalous-but-not-horrific things in the SCP list, and the guano excretion mechanism made me grin. +1 with my compliments.

item.title: Re: My first, be brutal!
item.content:encoded: Neat concept, Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. I'm a propeller-head, this is definitely odd, and the image of the plane happily bouncing up and down as the tether is attached pleases me on a deep level. I've always thought that planes would enjoy flying.

Am I the only one that imagines it making little sputtering engine-noise vocalisations as they're tying the tether on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great SCP, keep it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Twenty grams of diazepam is wildly excessive. A typical dose to sedate a 500kg horse would be about 100mg. Sopwith Triplanes weigh less than 500kg. 20000mg is far off what is necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never thought an airplane could be so cute :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually quite enjoyed this one. Nice touch with the glucosamine and condroitin for the osteoarthritis treatment (I missed the bit about the arthritis the first time I read through and incorrectly assumed you were just throwing drug names around). My only gripe would be to reclassify it as Euclid, but that's really up to you. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this because it was simple enough to be interesting, no convoluted plot twist or drawn out description to bore me. And I say, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm doubly interested in this skip because 1) It's simply written and gets to the point 2) I go to a vocational school, so the airplane aspect appeals to the future engineer in me. (Don't let me near this thing. I would probably dissect it).

All in all, it was an enjoyable read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a bird! It's a plane! It's a bird-plane!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bird-airplane-skip-thing. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's such a happy little plane. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am just happy it was not made of peoples.

Also, poop machine gun needs to getting written into the next tale possible! Seriously, would be great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I no-voted before the edit, way back in 2013, but now…

Gosh, it's adorable!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good to see this one on the featured article list. New members take heed: this is a biological anomaly done right.

Note the specifications of habitat and dietary requirements. Take heed of the subtle use of complex tissues and proteins to explain its shape. See how the dangerous nature of the object has nothing to do with a hatred of humans, and is simply a side effect of its predatory nature. Observe the relation between organism (falcon) and anomalous shape (a fighter plane) and how the two compliment rather than oppose one another.

Equally interesting: There's no back-story. There doesn't need to be one. The interest of the article (the "hook") is entirely in the realistic biological and behavioral description of the anomalous creature. No excessive feels-pulling. No blatant shoe-horned origin story. Just a weird critter played with a straight face and attention to detail. And that's enough.

This is the kind of "SCP Classic" feel that I want to see more of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think SCP articles are generally supposed to make me go 'd'awwww', but this one sure did. Cute, well-executed, and a nice breath of fresh air that will no doubt make the next horror SCP I read all the more horrifying. I also wonder which personnel it keeps trying to shoot its guano at, and what they did to piss it off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Articles like this are important. It would be ludicrous to assume all anomalies are affronts to the laws of physics, or otherwise make reality shit its pants. Some of them, arguably most of them, would be completely harmless and relatively mundane. This is a bird in the shape of a tri-plane. Its behavior, ect are entirely consistent with other birds, the only thing that makes it different being its shape. Not everything the Foundation contains is going to be a world-ender or otherwise ruin someones' day. Some of them are just going to be harmless curiosities, which are only contained because they do, technically, fit the description of "anomalous".

Just my 2 cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
all in all it is a tri-plane that is actually a bird
nothing that makes it interesting

I could just be biased, but I was pleasantly charmed by the concept, especially the handling of the general biological functions (eating, expelling of excrement, etc.) and the "behavior" of the object. I felt that the hook was in thinking of the machine in terms of a living animal; the airplane equivalents of avian features was interesting to think on.

also I found it adorable that the plane bounces up and down before flight sessions

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried, but I just can't get behind this as a full article. while the colour text does add to it, all in all it is a tri-plane that is actually a bird. Perhaps I have been jaded by the rest of the site, but for me there is no hook, nothing that makes it interesting, and therefore a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Upvoted. Just my opinion, but I think that the whole idea is nice.
Anomalous objects don't have to be extremely frightening or bizarre to be good.

Edit: I tried so hard not to sound like an expert (because I'm not one) and I think I just abjectly failed. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. This brought a little smile to my face as I read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Credit to the original author for doing a biological machine that looks completely normal, I like those.
I also like that it is just a normal bird that happens to be a plane. It isn't sapient or evil, just a bird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it seems to like the Foundation staff

except for the ones it shoots its poop at, of course

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. There are so many parts of this article that I like, especially

however, it will flinch when tissue samples are taken, tremble during thunderstorms, and bounce up and down when its tether is being attached prior to flight sessions.

At first I thought it was a little silly, but I was too overcome by daaaaws.

(also the concept was interesting. It's a bird, it's a plane…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D'awww. It's actually really cute. I like how it's not actively, you know, hateful. It's just a bird shaped like a plane, and it seems to like the Foundation staff. I mean, the thing came back on its own after stretching its wings for a spell. I can see an older gentleman petting it while giving it a meal, talking to it absent mindedly, the propeller twirling every so often because it's happy.

So many things in containment are obviously depressed, it's nice to see one every so often that shows we're not -trying- to make them miserable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loving the rewrite. +All the 1s

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dr. Smith? Some other random name/personal info?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seat belts, definitely seat belts.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Voct has posted his rewrite. I request a cancellation of the deletion vote. My vote brought the rating up to 0 (Zero).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading the rewrite I had a very vivid image of an interview with a wizened old handler in the documentary about the species. He pats its wing absentmindedly as he discusses the late-onset health issues which now plague the creature, how it slipped and broke a wheel last may- but that's all part of the life cycle, *restrained sniff*.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the rewrite much more. Dunno which is more disturbing imagery, Birdshit machine guns, or being slowly ripped apart and digested by a seatbelt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Okay, I upvoted the rewrite for the fact that it shoots it's droppings at people it doesn't like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. I like it, but it's just barely not good enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one still doesn't really do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really dig the rewrite. I bet you can do more with a back story, but I certainly think it is worth keeping around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rewrite done; this is now Accipiter sopwithii, pronounced "sop-WITH-ee-eye".

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Thirding deletion.

seems to have risen above threshold. vote stopped for now

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Per our rewrite rules, we can elect to give rewriters a deadline before deleting. Apparently Voct told Scan that he could have it done by Friday, so why not give him 'til then as a deadline? THEN delete.

But yeah, the lines at the top of the article aren't really needed.

And in the circumstance that Voct doesn't get his rewrite up before deadline, I also vote for deletion.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Suggesting deletion at -11.

Voct can repost it later if he wants. Stays of execution are not granted based off of lines at the top of a page, and there's nothing preventing him from posting to a fresh page when he's done.

item.title: Re: Idea
item.content:encoded: For the record, Voct has said that he can have it done by this time Friday. If it is not posted by then, I will allow staff to begin a deletion vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eagerly awaiting the rewrite; personally I like the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it's made entirely of organic materials…it isn't a world war one triplane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a bit cliche in concept, but I like the way this is done. I'm looking forward to the rewrite

item.title: Re: Idea
item.content:encoded: That's how I felt when I read it, too. However, I like his idea more than mine, so it's hard to call him on it. I try really hard not to see the negative side, so I decided to take it as a compliment that I inspired such…enthusiasm.

item.title: Re: Idea
item.content:encoded:
… I read all this as you patronizing the author, in a kind way…
… by the middle you were getting into the idea…

… by the end you were mostly talking to yourself, pondering details, then a kiddy 'can I can I?' XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to say, It was not a shameless self-insertion. It's just a safe-class object, so using an O5 seems overkill, and a more well known doctor's name is something I'm not comfortable using yet. Plus, a black boxed name seems like unnecessary censorship for a safe-class object, so I used my name. Does that seem justified? What would you have done?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its cool, but I cant help but think of the living room and the carnivorous water slide. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Filling it in would make much more sense. The Foundation isn't looking to make it's records PG-13, so the precise way in which the plane eats pilots would probably be described in as much detail as is necessary. You might want to read Zen And The Art of [DATA-EXPUNGED], it will clarify a lot about expungment, redaction, and blackboxes.
